summary:
The earliest accounting systems emerged in Mesopotamia during the [[event:1617|Uruk period]], approximately 3500–3000 BCE, driven by the administrative needs of large temple and palace institutions that managed agricultural surpluses, craft production, and labor. The earliest accounting tools were clay tokens of various shapes — spheres, cones, discs, cylinders — each representing a specific quantity of a specific commodity such as grain, livestock, or oil. These tokens were sometimes sealed within hollow clay balls (bullae) as a record of a transaction, with impressions of the tokens pressed on the outside of the bulla for reference.

The token system gradually gave way to flat clay tablets on which token shapes were impressed or incised to record inventories and transactions, and this transition is now recognized as a critical step in the [[event:1619|invention of writing]]. The [[event:1620|proto-cuneiform tablets]] from Uruk represent the direct documentary output of this accounting tradition. The invention of accounting systems thus represents not merely a bookkeeping innovation but a cognitive leap — the externalization of memory into a durable medium — that made possible the administrative complexity of the world's first cities and states.
